data.title: Supreme Court rules for Christian counselor in 'conversion therapy' ban case
data.description:
The Supreme Court sided with a Christian counselor on Tuesday in her free speech challenge to Colorado’s ban on counselors attempting to change a minor’s sexual orientation or gender identity. 

Justice Neil Gorsuch, writing for the 8-1 majority, said lower courts used too lenient a standard in upholding the ban.  

The law regulates counselors’ speech in an attempt to silence a certain viewpoint, Gorsuch wrote.

“Fortunately, that is not the world the First Amendment envisions for us,” the justice wrote.

The court’s decision is poised to have ripple effects across the country, with more than 20 states having enacted similar measures. But two of the court’s liberal justices, Elena Kagan and Sonia Sotomayor, signaled that states that write more tailored laws could still prevail. 

The court’s other liberal justice, Ketanji Brown Jackson, dissented alone. 

“To do anything else opens a dangerous can of worms,” Jackson wrote. “It threatens to impair States’ ability to regulate the provision of medical care in any respect. It extends the Constitution into uncharted territory in an utterly irrational fashion. And it ultimately risks grave harm to Americans’ health and wellbeing.” 

Passed in 2019, Colorado’s ban prohibits licensed mental health counselors from engaging in “any practice or treatment” that “attempts or purports to change” a minor’s sexual orientation or gender identity. Critics have described it as “conversion therapy.” Violations can carry $5,000 fines, and counselors can be suspended and stripped of their license. 

Kaley Chiles, a Christian and licensed Colorado-based counselor, sued under the First Amendment, arguing the state was trying to control her conversations with patients to suppress disfavored views on LGBTQ rights. 

A federal district judge and a divided appeals panel sided with Colorado, agreeing its law regulated not speech, but professional conduct.   

The Supreme Court sided with Chiles by agreeing the state’s law regulates speech and needs to clear a more stringent constitutional test. The justices sent the case back to the lower courts to apply it. 

In defending its law, Colorado pointed to major professional medical associations that suggest conversion therapy is ineffective and can be harmful to minors. The Williams Institute estimates that 698,000 U.S. adults have received conversion therapy, including 350,000 who did so as adolescents. 

Chiles is represented by Alliance Defending Freedom (ADF), a conservative Christian legal powerhouse that has regularly emerged victorious before the Supreme Court, including in previous challenges to Colorado laws. 

Their challenge was supported by outside briefs from Christian counseling and medical groups, the U.S. Conference of Catholic Bishops, The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, Catholic University of America, the Becket Fund for Religious Liberty and 20 Republican state attorneys general. 

The Trevor Project, PFLAG, the American Psychological Association, nearly 200 Democratic members of Congress and 20 Democratic state attorneys general urged the justices to side with Colorado. 

It’s not the only Supreme Court case this term implicating LGBTQ protections. The court is also weighing whether states can ban transgender girls from competing on women’s sports teams in schools.

data.title: O'Reilly: Trump 'needs a deal with Iran' within 6 weeks
data.description:
Conservative commentator Bill O’Reilly on Monday said President Trump needs to strike a deal with Iran “within six weeks,” warning that the conflict’s effect on rising prices could hurt Republican control of Congress in the November midterm elections.

O’Reilly raised this point in his appearance on “On Balance with Leland Vittert” on NewsNation, The Hill’s sister network. Vittert reiterated a prior point O’Reilly previously made that Trump was “up against a deadline” for prices to drop in April or May in order to see a favorable outcome during the midterms or risk his political future should the GOP lose its hold on Congress.

“I think the president –– I don’t want to use the word desperately, but [he] needs a deal with Iran within, I’d say, six weeks,” O’Reilly said. “And then oil prices will come down very very quickly because it’s a glut oil, worldwide. There’s a lot of oil.”

O’Reilly said that Trump knows “that if things continue to be a stalemate like Ukraine, very similar, that he could lose the midterms.” He later added that Trump knows not to “negotiate a deal out of desperation.”

“He needs a deal that would work to his advantage in every way,” the former Fox News host said. “And he may get one. It just depends on how much damage the Iranians can absorb. And they’ve absorbed a lot of damage right now. And it also depends on the Persian people, so when is enough enough for them?”

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), a war hawk and Trump ally, on Monday said he encouraged the president to “wind down” the conflict and “wind up” efforts for a peace agreement. Congressional Republicans, who have largely backed the conflict, have signaled that its impact could be felt with the outcome of the midterms.

Graham, however, has come under fire for public comments that seemed to suggest support for boots on the ground in Iran. Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.) advocated in a post on X last week against “Washington’s war machine” leading the U.S. to repeat the outcome of the Iraq War but in Iran.

“And yes, when we say Washington’s war machine, we mean Lindsey Graham,” she added.

The Trump administration said it has held talks with Iranian officials through Pakistani mediators, having previously submitted a 15-point peace plan to end the military offensive. Iran rejected the offer and submitted its own peace plan, but Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baqaei said Monday that his country has not negotiated with the U.S. over ending the conflict.

Baqaei added that Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi’s position is “very clear” that as long as the U.S.’s “military aggression and invasion continue with full intensity, all our efforts and capabilities are devoted to defending Iran’s essence.”

data.title: Live updates: Hegseth, Trump push US allies to help reopen Strait of Hormuz as gas prices hit $4
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told reporters on Tuesday morning, in the fifth week of the Iran conflict, that regime change “has occurred” in Iran and echoed the president in pushing U.S. allies to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

President Trump on Tuesday lashed out at allies over their reluctance to help secure Iran’s Strait of Hormuz, telling them to either buy U.S. oil or get it themselves from the critical waterway. 

“All of those countries that can’t get jet fuel because of the Strait of Hormuz, like the United Kingdom, which refused to get involved in the decapitation of Iran, I have a suggestion for you: Number 1, buy from the U.S., we have plenty, and Number 2, build up some delayed courage, go to the Strait, and just TAKE IT,” Trump wrote in a TruthSocial post. 

The Supreme Court on Tuesday issued an opinion siding 8-1 with a Christian counselor in her free speech fight against Colorado’s “conversion therapy” ban for minors.

The standoff over Department of Homeland Security funding is also on display, underscoring the divides between House and Senate GOP leaders. House Republicans, meanwhile, are looking to set up another reconciliation package funding federal immigration operations and the war in Iran, which recently hit its one-month anniversary.

Trump on Tuesday will attend a series of meetings before attending the musical “Chicago” at the Kennedy Center in the evening.

Follow live below.

data.title: Hezbollah inspired man who crashed into Michigan synagogue: FBI
data.description:
A man who crashed his car into a synagogue in Michigan earlier this month was motivated by Hezbollah, FBI special agent in charge Jennifer Runyan said on Monday.

Runyan, the head of the bureau’s Detroit field office, told reporters that “based on the evidence gathered to date, we assess this attack to be a Hezbollah-inspired act of terrorism purposely targeting the Jewish community and the largest Jewish Temple in Michigan.”

She noted that Ayman Ghazali, 41, began “in earnest” to plan his attack on Temple Israel in West Bloomfield Township, Mich., on March 9 — three days before carrying it out. However, Runyan added that a review of Ghazali’s online activity since January reveals a “recurring” search history of pro-Hezbollah news channels, Iranian news channels and videos about shootouts and bullets.

On March 12, Ghazali drove his Ford F-150 into the hallway of an early childhood education area at the facility, hitting a security guard. After he exchanged gunfire with another guard, the suspect died by a self-inflicted gunshot wound. No other individuals were injured.  

On the morning of March 9, Ghazali viewed webpages of local synagogues, Runyan explained. That afternoon, he tried to purchase a weapon from two different individuals, whom FBI investigators have interviewed. 

After those individuals denied his request, the man purchased an AR-style rifle, 10 rifle magazines and roughly 300 rounds of .223 rifle ammunition from a gun store in Dearborn, Mich. While at the gun store, he ordered a rifle magazine pouch and 40 5.3-gallon water containers from an online store.

Throughout the night of March 9 and the early morning of March 10, Ghazali viewed webpages of Jewish cultural, educational and religious centers in the Detroit metro area. In the afternoon on March 10, he practiced with his new gun at a local shooting range and later purchased more than $2,200 worth of fireworks.

On March 11, he posted photos of his deceased brothers, niece and nephew and exchanged messages with his family. His family members were killed in an Israeli airstrike in Lebanon on March 5, with the Israeli military saying his brother was a commander in Hezbollah — a militant organization backed by Iran that was designated as a terrorist group by the U.S. in 1997. 

Runyan noted, though, that Ghazali was not the subject of a prior FBI investigation and was not on the terrorist watchlist. 

Early on March 12, before carrying out the attack, Ghazali posted photos of his deceased family members on Facebook and wrote, “We will seek retribution for their sacred blood,” “Israel is a cancerous/malignant growth” along with “And the oppressors will soon know what kind of end they meet,” among other comments.

“We are very grateful that, aside from the assailant, there were no additional fatalities,” Runyan said. “However, this evidence makes very clear the attacker intended to cause a significant amount of harm to the members and children present at Temple Israel that day.”

“At this stage of the investigation, we have continued to find no evidence of co-conspirators, nor do we have information that indicate a current active threat to the community at this time,” he added.

data.title: Trump slams France for not allowing plane to fly over to Israel
data.description:
President Trump criticized France on Tuesday for being “very unhelpful” in the Iran war after the country would not allow planes headed to Israel to fly over its territory.

“The Country of France wouldn’t let planes headed to Israel, loaded up with military supplies, fly over French territory,” Trump wrote on Truth Social. “France has been VERY UNHELPFUL with respect to the ‘Butcher of Iran,’ who has been successfully eliminated!”

“The U.S.A. will remember!!!” he wrote.

Sources told Reuters that the refusal happened over the weekend, and that it was the first time France has rejected air space since the beginning of the Iran operation.

It comes after Italy reportedly would not allow the U.S. to land war planes at a base in Sicily and Spain closed its airspace to U.S. planes.

“This was made perfectly clear to the American military and forces from the very beginning. Therefore, neither the bases are authorized, nor, of course, is the use of Spanish airspace authorized for any actions related to the war in Iran,” Spain’s Defense Minister Margarita Robles told reporters Monday, according to The Associated Press.

Trump also hit on other U.S. allies in a separate post, naming specifically the United Kingdom.

“All of those countries that can’t get jet fuel because of the Strait of Hormuz, like the United Kingdom, which refused to get involved in the decapitation of Iran, I have a suggestion for you: Number 1, buy from the U.S., we have plenty, and Number 2, build up some delayed courage, go to the Strait, and just TAKE IT,” he said.

“You’ll have to start learning how to fight for yourself, the U.S.A. won’t be there to help you anymore, just like you weren’t there for us,” he wrote.

data.title: Nate Silver on recent polling: Trump has 'profound problems'
data.description:
Pollster Nate Silver said President Trump has “profound problems” as the president’s job approval rating took a significant hit in recent surveys. 

Silver posted a string of posts on social platform X about the downward trend, after Trump’s average approval rating fell below 40 percentage points last week. 

Two separate surveys released last week by The Associated Press-NORC Research Center and Quinnipiac University found a 38 percent approval rating for the president, while a University of Massachusetts Amherst survey reported Trump’s lowest approval rating yet, 33 percent.

A Fox News poll released last Wednesday found that 41 percent of respondents supported how the president has handled his job. A Harvard-sponsored poll from last week pinned his approval rating at 43 percent. 

This dip comes amid the Trump administration’s ongoing military operations against Iran. The U.S. and Israel launched joint strikes against the country on Feb. 28 after the two sides failed to reach an agreement on a new nuclear deal. 

Iranian counterstrikes in the Gulf region have effectively halted the passage of oil-carrying ships through the Strait of Hormuz, a major oil trading corridor in the global economy. The price of international benchmark Brent crude oil has skyrocketed, hitting a trade value of around $118 a barrel on Tuesday morning. 

Prior to the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, Brent crude oil was trading around $70 a barrel. 

The price of gas has also increased. In the U.S., the national average price of standard gas hit $4 a gallon on Tuesday morning, up over a dollar from a month ago, according to AAA. 

While Silver acknowledged that rising fuel prices resulting from the conflict are a “big factor” in the president’s approval ratings, he wrote that there are also “signs of erosion among his base.” 

“Only 22% of Americans have a *strongly* favorable view of Trump,” the pollster wrote on X. “2028 aspirants are starting to pull away from him. One wonders about the effect of his age, too.”

The economy is expected to play a big role in the upcoming midterm elections in November, with House Democrats focusing on a message of affordability in their effort to win back control of the chamber. 

Trump largely ran on promises to address cost of living concerns during his 2024 presidential campaign. However, the economic hits resulting from the military campaign against Iran and inflation in the U.S. have been a particular weak point for the president in recent surveys.

data.title: Russian oil tanker docks in Cuba despite US blockade
data.description:
A Russian oil tanker docked in Cuba on Tuesday despite a U.S. blockade on shipments to the country as the Trump administration pushes for regime change in Havana.

The Anatoly Kolodkin vessel is carrying 730,000 barrels of crude oil and is likely to meet the Cuban population’s daily demand for nine to ten days, according to The Associated Press.

“Our gratitude to the Government and People of #Russia for all the support we are receiving. Valuable shipment that arrives amid the complex energy situation we are facing,” Cuba’s Minister of Energy and Mines Vicente de la O Levy wrote in a post on X.

President Trump cleared the shipment on Sunday, noting that it wouldn’t help Cuba much nor would it bolster earnings for Russian President Vladimir Putin.

“It doesn’t help him. He loses one boatload of oil, that’s all it is. If he wants to do that, and if other countries want to do it, that doesn’t bother me much. It’s not going to have an impact,” Trump told reporters on Sunday night aboard Air Force One.   

“Cuba’s finished,” he added. “They have a bad regime. They have very bad and corrupt leadership. And whether or not they get a boat of oil, it’s not going to matter. I prefer letting it in, whether it’s Russia or anybody else, because the people need heat and cooling and all of the other things that you need.” 

However, the resources should help Cubans who rely on imported oil for energy, cooking, heating and cooling alongside other necessities. Energy imports from Venezuela were effectively halted after the Trump administration captured Nicolás Maduro. 

Francisco Pichón, the United Nations Resident Coordinator in Cuba, said 114,080 people with disabilities and 2,500 caregivers face barriers to vital services due to the blockade, while noting critical blocks to food production and that 28 percent of children are facing reduced school schedules.

On Monday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said the administration may continue to allow oil shipments from vessels on a “case-by-case basis,” but still reserves the right to “seize vessels” that violate U.S. sanctions.

“Cuba’s non-functional economy cannot be fixed unless they undergo dramatic, political and leadership change, but there has been no formal change,” Leavitt told reporters.

data.title: Immigration could cost Republicans the midterms — here's what they should do
data.description:
A recent Reuters-Ipsos poll indicates that Republicans could benefit in this year’s midterm elections from the Trump administration’s “rule of law” immigration stance. However, many people disapprove of the aggressive enforcement methods being used by Customs and Border Protection and Immigration and Customs Enforcement to implement those policies. This is directed primarily at the use of force.   

This is a problem for the Republican Party, already facing strong challenges from Democrats, who have been overperforming since President Trump began his second term. The president’s party historically has lost ground in midterm elections, in 20 out of 22 elections since 1938; nationwide, Democrats have flipped 30 legislative seats since last year.

This doesn’t mean that the administration should change its policies — it means that it should dial down its tactics. It also would help to show that they are just enforcing the law, and that they are not the xenophobic, anti-immigrant racists that the Democrats say they are.   

The following suggestions would help to achieve these objectives. 

Establish a program for accepting and evaluating complaints of excessive violence

YouGov polling indicates that 90 percent of Democrats, 60 percent of independents and 30 percent of Republicans think that ICE sometimes or often uses unnecessary force. And people who think they have been the victim of excessive violence have little recourse other than going to court. The administration can mitigate this problem by establishing a forum for considering such complaints and holding ICE officers accountable if do use excessive force. 

Don’t rush to judgement

Former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem rushed to judgment on Jan. 7, when an ICE officer shot and killed Renee Good in Minneapolis. Instead of waiting for the results of an investigation, she immediately asserted at a press conference later that day that the shooting had been justified. 

According to Vice President JD Vance, the ICE officer who killed Good “is protected by absolute immunity. … He was doing his job.” And Stephen Miller, a White House deputy chief of staff, posted a clip on social media telling ICE officers, “You have immunity to perform your duties, and no one … can prevent you from fulfilling your legal obligations and duties.” 

That may be, but it didn’t eliminate the need for an investigation before deciding whether Good’s killing was justified. An investigation was needed to determine whether the ICE officer was in fact “doing his job” when he shot Good, whether he needed to kill Good to fulfill his “legal obligations and duties” and whether killing Good violated any federal laws or internal ICE guidelines.

In any case, the issue politically isn’t whether the ICE officer who killed Good has immunity from prosecution. It’s whether the Department of Homeland Security is serious about determining whether ICE officers are using excessive force. 

Propose and advocate legalization programs

Republicans should consider renewing the four-pillar proposal Trump offered during his first term, which would have provided lawful status for undocumented aliens in the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals program. The first pillar was the legalization program; the second required an end to chain migration. The other two pillars were building a border wall and ending the Diversity Visa Program.

The only deal-killer was chain migration. But that problem could have been avoided by offering DACA participants legal status through the Special Immigrant Juveniles program. Under this program, provisions state that “no natural parent or prior adoptive parent of any alien provided special immigrant status … shall thereafter, by virtue of such parentage, be accorded any right, privilege, or status under this act.”

Another option for Republicans is to update and amend the registry provision in the Immigration and Nationality Act of 1952. The Registry Act of 1929 established a process for immigrants to create a record of lawful admission for permanent residence. To be eligible for this program, they had to establish that they entered the U.S. before 1921, maintained continuous presence since then, and met other eligibility requirements. Congress hasn’t updated the registry provision since 1986, when the cutoff year was changed to 1972.

The new registry date would be decided through negotiations in Congress and establish a new type of lawful status that I call “revocable lawful status.” This would make it lawful for the aliens to live and work in the U.S., but it would not provide a path toward lawful permanent resident status or citizenship. Derivative status would be limited to spouses and children. Eligibility would be limited to long-term residents with no criminal convictions other than offenses based on their illegal entry into the country, and waivers of this requirement would be prohibited. It is unlikely that longtime residents without criminal records will ever be put into removal proceedings anyway — no one benefits from keeping them in unlawful status.

At the very least, the administration should monitor the use of violence and take officers suspected of excessive violence out of the field.   

Nolan Rappaport was detailed to the House Judiciary Committee as an Executive Branch Immigration Law Expert for three years. He subsequently served as an immigration counsel for the Subcommittee on Immigration, Border Security and Claims for four years. Prior to working on the Judiciary Committee, he wrote decisions for the Board of Immigration Appeals for 20 years.

data.title: Lawyers for accused Charlie Kirk assassin: ATF couldn't conclusively connect bullet, rifle
data.description:
The lawyers of Tyler Robinson, the man charged in connection with the shooting death of conservative activist Charlie Kirk, on Friday requested a six-month delay for a court hearing scheduled in May to review evidence that suggests the bullet that killed Kirk did not match the rifle.

Kirk, one of Turning Point USA’s founders, was fatally shot in the neck while speaking at a rally at Utah Valley University in Orem, Utah, on Sept. 10. Prosecutors are seeking the death penalty for Robinson, 22, who was charged with aggravated murder. He has not yet entered a plea.

Robinson’s lawyers wrote in the recent court filing to District Judge Tony Graf in Utah that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) “was unable to identify the bullet recovered at autopsy to the rifle allegedly tied to Mr. Robinson.” They added that the FBI is conducting a second comparative bullet analysis and a lead bullet analysis, but neither has been completed.

“Again, until the defense receives the case files and protocols relating to these analyses, and has had them independently evaluated by its expert, they will not be in a position to assess the reliablity of this evidence if either the State or the defense decides to proffer it,” the defense team added.

It said that based on what the final reports show, “the defense may very well decide to offer the testimony of the ATF firearm analyst as exculpatory evidence.”

Robinson’s lawyers wrote that the analysis results could clear their client of blame at his preliminary hearing, but they need time to review the results of the completed testing. The defense team cited parts of other public findings with inconclusive results.

Ballistics analysts are looking for microscopic markings left on the bullet as it passes through the gun’s barrel, similar to fingerprints, The Associated Press reported.

Robinson’s lawyers also wrote that they need 60 days to determine what could be missing from an initial review of digital evidence. They received a hard drive with 31 hours of audio files, 700 hours of video and 600,000 data files.

The next hearing is scheduled for April 17 for a defense motion to ban cameras from the courtroom.

data.title: Lindsey Graham responds after photos showing him at Disney World
data.description:
Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) responded to photos of him at Disney World over the weekend in the midst of a partial government shutdown. 

Graham was seen at the theme park in Florida on Friday and Saturday with a bubble wand while waiting in line to get on the “Space Mountain” ride. 

“I was invited to a meeting in South Florida on Friday with Trump official Steve Witkoff … to talk about the possibility of normalization between Saudi Arabia and Israel,” Graham told TMZ. 

“I went to Orlando to meet friends after. I’m already back in South Carolina,” he added, urging the outlet to call a Democrat to discuss the shutdown. 

Images of Graham emerged after TMZ requested photos of lawmakers away from Washington during the shutdown. 

On Monday, the South Carolina senator posted a picture of him hunting with a gun in Edgefield County, S.C., after receiving pushback for vacationing while Transportation Security Administration (TSA) staff are being asked to work without pay. 

Graham’s office did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment on the matter.

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS), which houses TSA, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), U.S. Border Patrol and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, among other agencies, has been shut down for over 45 days. 

The House and Senate are currently in a standoff over whether to fund immigration agencies as Democrats push for reforms following the deaths of two U.S. citizens at the hands of federal authorities.
